============================================================

============================================================
REASON FOR ADMISSION / PRESENTING COMPLAINT
============================================================
Mr. James Wilson, a 67-year-old male with a known history of ischaemic cardiomyopathy,
CKD Stage 3b, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, and hypertension, presented to the Emergency
Department at 02:14 on 09 February 2024 via ambulance, accompanied by his wife.
Chief complaints on arrival:
- Progressive worsening of dyspnoea over the preceding 5 days, now present at rest
- Orthopnoea — unable to lie flat; sleeping upright in recliner for 3 nights
- Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea — 2 episodes in the past week
- Bilateral lower limb oedema, worsening over 10 days, now extending to the knees
- Weight gain of 6.2 kg over the past 2 weeks (confirmed by home scale records)
- Reduced urine output for the past 3 days
- Fatigue and generalised weakness — unable to walk more than 10 metres without stopping
- Mild chest heaviness, non-radiating, without diaphoresis — attributed to dyspnoea
- No fever, cough, or recent upper respiratory tract infection
Mrs. Wilson reported that her husband had been non-compliant with his fluid restriction
(2L/day) and had consumed significantly higher-than-usual sodium intake over the preceding
2 weeks during a family gathering period. He had also self-discontinued furosemide
approximately 10 days prior due to concerns about urinary urgency affecting his ability
to attend social events.

Vital Signs:
- Temperature:       37.4°C (tympanic)
- Heart Rate:        102 bpm — irregularly irregular
- Blood Pressure:    198/114 mmHg (right arm, sitting)

- Respiratory Rate:  26 breaths per minute
- Oxygen Saturation: 88% on room air; 96% on 4L/min nasal cannula
- Weight:            98.4 kg (dry weight estimated 92 kg)
- Height:            175 cm
- BMI:               32.1 kg/m2
General:
Patient is visibly dyspnoeic at rest, sitting upright and leaning forward. Mildly
distressed, unable to speak in full sentences. Pale but not cyanosed peripherally.
Alert and orientated to time, place, and person.
Cardiovascular:
- JVP markedly elevated — estimated at 12 cm above sternal angle
- Apex beat displaced to anterior axillary line, 6th intercostal space
- Heart sounds: S1 and S2 present; S3 gallop audible at apex (consistent with volume overload)
- Grade II/VI pansystolic murmur at apex, radiating to axilla — consistent with
  functional mitral regurgitation
- Peripheral pulses: present but weak bilaterally in lower limbs
Respiratory:
- Bilateral coarse crackles to mid-zones on auscultation
- Dullness to percussion at right base, consistent with pleural effusion
- Accessory muscle use present
- No wheeze

- Hepatomegaly — liver edge palpable 4 cm below right costal margin, tender

- Shifting dullness — mild ascites not excluded
- Bowel sounds present and normal
Extremities:
- Bilateral pitting oedema 3+ extending to knees
- Skin warm and dry in upper limbs; cool and mottled in lower limbs
- No clubbing; no palmar erythema

Neurological:
- Alert, orientated, GCS 15/15

--- ELECTROCARDIOGRAM (09 Feb 2024, 02:30) ---
Rate: 102 bpm
Rhythm: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Axis: Left axis deviation
PR interval: Not applicable (AF)
QRS duration: 148 ms — Left Bundle Branch Block morphology (LBBB), pre-existing
ST/T changes: ST depression in V4-V6, likely rate-related and in context of LBBB
QTc: 472 ms
Impression: Atrial fibrillation with RVR. LBBB (pre-existing per prior ECGs). Rate-related
ST changes. No acute STEMI equivalent identifiable.
--- CHEST X-RAY (09 Feb 2024, 02:45) ---
- Cardiomegaly — cardiothoracic ratio 0.62
- Bilateral perihilar haziness — consistent with pulmonary oedema
- Kerley B lines present bilaterally
- Right-sided pleural effusion — moderate
- Left-sided pleural effusion — small
- No pneumothorax; no consolidation to suggest pneumonia
--- ECHOCARDIOGRAM (10 Feb 2024) ---
Performed by Dr. Carlos Mendez (Cardiology)
Left Ventricle:
- Severely dilated LV (LVEDD 7.2 cm)
- Global hypokinesis with anteroseptal akinesis (consistent with prior LAD territory infarct)
- LVEF: 30% (Simpson's biplane method) — severely reduced (previously 28% in Aug 2023)
- LV diastolic dysfunction: Grade III (restrictive filling pattern)
Right Ventricle:
- Mildly dilated RV
- Mildly reduced RV systolic function — TAPSE 16 mm
- Estimated RVSP 42 mmHg — mild pulmonary hypertension

lic dysfunction: Grade III (restrictive filling pattern)
Right Ventricle:
- Mildly dilated RV
- Mildly reduced RV systolic function — TAPSE 16 mm
- Estimated RVSP 42 mmHg — mild pulmonary hypertension
Valves:
- Mitral valve: Moderate functional mitral regurgitation (regurgitant volume 38 mL/beat)
- Aortic valve: Trileaflet, mild aortic sclerosis, no significant stenosis or regurgitation
- Tricuspid valve: Mild tricuspid regurgitation
- Pulmonary valve: Normal
Other:
- IVC dilated at 2.4 cm, non-collapsible — consistent with elevated RAP
- No pericardial effusion
- No intracardiac thrombus identified
Impression: Severely reduced LVEF at 30%. Dilated ischaemic cardiomyopathy. Moderate
functional MR. Mild pulmonary hypertension. Features consistent with decompensated HFrEF.
--- LABORATORY RESULTS (SUMMARY) ---

============================================================

============================================================
--- DAY 1-2: STABILISATION PHASE ---
Mr. Wilson was admitted directly to the Coronary Care Unit (CCU) given haemodynamic
instability, oxygen desaturation, and hyperkalemia.
Immediate management:
Hyperkalemia (K+ 6.1):
- Calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% solution IV administered for cardiac membrane
  stabilisation — repeat ECG post-administration showed no peaked T-waves, confirming
  no immediate lethal arrhythmia risk
- Insulin-dextrose infusion (10 units actrapid in 50 mL 50% dextrose over 30 minutes)
- Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma) 10g orally three times daily commenced for
  ongoing potassium removal
- Ramipril held given AKI and hyperkalemia
- Eplerenone held given hyperkalemia
Acute Decompensated Heart Failure:
- Furosemide 80mg IV bolus administered on arrival, then 40mg IV twice daily
- Fluid restriction: 1.5L per 24 hours
- Sodium restriction: <2g per day — dietary consult ordered
- Daily weights commenced (target: negative fluid balance of 1-1.5L per day)
- Strict fluid balance charting
- Supplemental oxygen: initially 4L/min nasal cannula, weaned to room air by Day 3

ordered
- Daily weights commenced (target: negative fluid balance of 1-1.5L per day)
- Strict fluid balance charting
- Supplemental oxygen: initially 4L/min nasal cannula, weaned to room air by Day 3
Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response:
- Rate control strategy adopted given chronic AF (patient has known paroxysmal AF,
  now appears to be persistent)
- Carvedilol dose uptitrated cautiously — held initially given haemodynamic instability,
  reintroduced Day 2 at 6.25mg BD
- Digoxin 0.25mg IV loading dose given on Day 1, followed by 0.0625mg oral daily
  (renally adjusted given AKI) — achieved rate control with HR 68-78 bpm by Day 3
- Anticoagulation: LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg SC daily, renally adjusted) commenced
  for AF; transitioned to apixaban 2.5mg BD at discharge (dose-reduced for age,
  weight, and renal function — meets 2-of-3 criteria for dose reduction)
Hypertension:
- IV labetalol avoided given decompensated HF
- Hypertension managed conservatively with diuresis; BP responded to decongestion
- Amlodipine 5mg orally once daily added Day 3 for residual hypertension
  (cautious use given oedema; justified by BP 168/96 despite diuresis)

nservatively with diuresis; BP responded to decongestion
- Amlodipine 5mg orally once daily added Day 3 for residual hypertension
  (cautious use given oedema; justified by BP 168/96 despite diuresis)
Blood Glucose Management:
- Metformin HELD on admission (AKI + contrast study planned)
- Insulin glargine continued at 28 units; correction doses of insulin aspart adjusted
- Endocrinology contacted — recommended increasing basal insulin to 32 units and
  optimising meal-time insulin with carbohydrate counting education
- Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM — FreeStyle Libre) reviewed by diabetes nurse
  educator on Day 3
AKI Management:
- Nephrology consulted (Dr. Sunita Patel) — AKI attributed to cardiorenal syndrome
  type 1 (acute decompensated HF causing AKI) with a prerenal component from
  self-discontinuation of diuretics and reduced renal perfusion
- Nephrotoxins avoided: NSAIDs, contrast (planned CT deferred), aminoglycosides
- Ramipril held — to be restarted cautiously once creatinine stabilises post-discharge
- Renally-adjusted dosing reviewed for all medications by clinical pharmacist (Dr. Brian Lee)
- Digoxin level checked Day 3: 0.9 ng/mL (therapeutic range 0.5-0.9; acceptable)
--- DAY 3-5: DIURESIS AND MONITORING PHASE ---

Brian Lee)
- Digoxin level checked Day 3: 0.9 ng/mL (therapeutic range 0.5-0.9; acceptable)
--- DAY 3-5: DIURESIS AND MONITORING PHASE ---
Response to IV diuresis:
- Day 1 urine output: 2,100 mL (net negative balance -800 mL)
- Day 2 urine output: 2,680 mL (net negative balance -1,400 mL)
- Day 3 urine output: 2,420 mL (net negative balance -1,200 mL)
- Weight trend: 98.4 kg → 97.1 → 95.3 → 93.8 kg
Respiratory improvement noted by Day 3 — crackles reduced to lung bases only.
Supplemental oxygen weaned and discontinued. Patient able to speak in full sentences
and walk to bathroom without significant dyspnoea.
Potassium normalised to 5.1 mEq/L by Day 3. Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate reduced
to once daily maintenance dosing.
Creatinine peaked at 264 umol/L (Day 2), then began to trend downward consistent
with improving cardiac output and renal perfusion.
Repeat ECG Day 3: AF with controlled ventricular rate (72 bpm). LBBB unchanged.
No new ST changes. QTc 458 ms.

Repeat ECG Day 3: AF with controlled ventricular rate (72 bpm). LBBB unchanged.
No new ST changes. QTc 458 ms.
Cardiology review (Dr. Mendez, Day 4):
- LVEF of 30% represents modest improvement from 28% (Aug 2023) — possible
  contribution of rate control reducing tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy
- Cardiac resynchronisation therapy (CRT) discussed — patient meets criteria:
  LBBB morphology, QRS >150 ms, LVEF <35%, NYHA Class III despite optimal
  medical therapy
- Decision: optimise medical therapy first for 3 months post-discharge and reassess
  LVEF. If EF remains <35%, proceed with CRT-D implantation.
- ICD discussion documented: patient counselled re: sudden cardiac death risk;
  wearable cardioverter defibrillator (LifeVest) considered as bridge — patient
  declined after counselling; decision documented and respected
--- DAY 5-7: TRANSITION TO ORAL DIURESIS ---
IV furosemide transitioned to oral furosemide 80mg twice daily (increased from
pre-admission dose of 40mg once daily).
Ramipril: not restarted (creatinine 184 umol/L on Day 5 — too elevated to safely
restart; plan to commence at low dose (1.25mg) as outpatient once creatinine
returns to near-baseline).
Eplerenone: restarted at 25mg once daily on Day 6 once potassium normalised
to 4.9 mEq/L and creatinine trending down.
Metformin: decision made NOT to restart at discharge given eGFR 41 mL/min/1.73m2
(below threshold of 45 for safe recommencement); to be reviewed by GP/endocrinologist
when eGFR recovers.

Metformin: decision made NOT to restart at discharge given eGFR 41 mL/min/1.73m2
(below threshold of 45 for safe recommencement); to be reviewed by GP/endocrinologist
when eGFR recovers.
Blood pressure well-controlled on carvedilol 12.5mg BD + amlodipine 5mg OD:
readings 128-138/76-84 mmHg.
Dietitian review completed Day 5:
- Low-sodium diet education (<2g sodium per day)
- Fluid restriction reinforced (1.5L per day)
- Heart-healthy, low-glycaemic index dietary education
- Written information provided in large print
Social work assessment Day 6:
- No immediate social needs
- Wife fully engaged as carer and medication supervisor
- Home services not required at this stage
- Referred to community heart failure nurse programme
--- DAY 7-8: DISCHARGE PLANNING ---
Patient clinically improved:
- Euvolaemic — no peripheral oedema, clear lung fields
- Dyspnoea resolved at rest; mild exertional dyspnoea on walking 100+ metres (NYHA II)
- HR well-controlled (68-74 bpm in AF)
- BP 132/78 mmHg
- eGFR 41 mL/min/1.73m2 (improving)
- Potassium 4.7 mEq/L
- BNP improved (840 pg/mL from 2840 pg/mL)
- Weight 92.6 kg (6 kg below admission weight — target dry weight confirmed 92 kg)

- BP 132/78 mmHg
- eGFR 41 mL/min/1.73m2 (improving)
- Potassium 4.7 mEq/L
- BNP improved (840 pg/mL from 2840 pg/mL)
- Weight 92.6 kg (6 kg below admission weight — target dry weight confirmed 92 kg)
Discharge education session conducted by nursing staff and pharmacist:
- All medications reviewed with patient and wife — pill organiser arranged
- Signs and symptoms of worsening HF requiring immediate presentation:
  weight gain >2 kg in 2 days, increasing dyspnoea, orthopnoea, ankle swelling
- Daily weighing instructions: same time each morning, after voiding, before eating
- Action plan provided in writing — laminated card given to patient
- Alcohol and fluid restriction reinforced
CPAP compliance discussed: Sleep Medicine nurse contacted; appointment arranged
within 4 weeks. Reinforced importance of CPAP for cardiac outcomes.
============================================================

MEDICATIONS HELD / CEASED:
- Metformin 500mg — CEASED at discharge; eGFR 41 mL/min/1.73m2, below safe threshold.
  To be reviewed by GP when eGFR >45 mL/min/1.73m2.
- Ramipril 5mg — HELD; creatinine not at safe level to restart. To be restarted at
  low dose (1.25mg) as outpatient under GP/cardiology supervision once creatinine
  approaches baseline.
============================================================
FOLLOW-UP PLAN
============================================================

text:

   - Consider restarting Metformin if eGFR >45 mL/min/1.73m2

section_title: 1. GENERAL PRACTITIONER (Dr. Helen Marsh, Greenhill Family Practice):

text:
- 6 weeks post-discharge (week of 25 March 2024)
   - Repeat echocardiogram to reassess LVEF
   - Discussion re: CRT-D candidacy if LVEF remains <35%
   - Review of AF management and anticoagulation
   - Uptitrate carvedilol if tolerated (target 25mg BD)
section_title: 2. CARDIOLOGY (Dr. Carlos Mendez, Riverside Cardiac Clinic):

text:
- 4 weeks post-discharge (week of 11 March 2024)
   - Review AKI resolution and CKD trajectory
   - Guidance on RAAS therapy reinstatement
   - UACR follow-up (was 680 mg/g — markedly elevated)
section_title: 3. NEPHROLOGY (Dr. Sunita Patel, Renal Outpatient Clinic):

text:
- 4 weeks post-discharge
   - Review insulin regimen and glycaemic control
   - Consider SGLT2 inhibitor once eGFR allows and AKI resolved — has dual benefit
     for HF and DKD (discuss at endocrine review)
   - Ongoing CGM review

- Phone call within 48 hours of discharge to confirm patient wellbeing
   - Home visit within 1 week
   - Weekly contact for first month

- Community dietitian referral sent; appointment within 3 weeks
============================================================

============================================================
The following topics were discussed with Mr. Wilson and Mrs. Wilson during the
inpatient stay, with written materials provided:

Patient demonstrated understanding of key points. Wife will assist with medication
organisation and daily weight recording.
============================================================
SAFETY NETTING / RED FLAG SYMPTOMS
============================================================
Patient and family instructed to present to Emergency Department or call 000 if:
- Weight gain greater than 2 kg over 2 days
- Worsening breathlessness at rest or lying flat
- New or increasing ankle/leg swelling
- Palpitations with dizziness, presyncope, or syncope
- Chest pain or pressure
- Reduced or absent urine output
- Blood in urine, stool, or unusual bleeding (given new anticoagulation with apixaban)
- Confusion or sudden deterioration in mental state
- Blood glucose consistently above 15 mmol/L or below 4 mmol/L with symptoms
============================================================
